coredata.dc:title: N, S co-doped carbon dots with orange luminescence synthesized through polymerization and carbonization reaction of amino acids
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zeng, Ya-Wen
Ma, De-Kun
Wang, Wei
Chen, Jing-Jing
Zhou, Lin
Zheng, Yi-Zhou
Yu, Kang
Huang, Shao-Ming
coredata.dc:description: For practical application, it is highly desirable to obtain carbon dots (CDs) through environmentally benign synthetic route, using green raw materials. On the other hand, at present, most of CDs reported in the literature showed blue, green and yellow emission. Therefore it is still necessary to develop new strategy to obtain CDs with longer wavelength emission in order to expand their application range. Toward this end, in this study, N, S co-doped CDs were synthesized through one-pot condensation polymerization and carbonization reactions under hydrothermal conditions, using two different amino acids as raw materials. Taking the reaction of l-serine with l-cystine as an example, the as-obtained products were characterized by various techniques such as transmission electron microscopy, elemental analysis, Fourier-transform infrared spectrum, X-ray photoelectron spectra, and so on. Interestingly, N, S co-doped CDs displayed unique orange emission at room temperature. The possible photoluminescence mechanism of N, S co-doped CDs was proposed. Furthermore, the as-synthesized N, S co-doped CDs were directly applied in the imaging of peritoneal macrophages of mice.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Doping
Hydrothermal synthesis
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N, S co-doped CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the formation process of N, S co-doped CDs.
(a) Panoramic TEM image of N, S co-doped CDs. (b) The statistical size distribution based on 100 randomly selected nanoparticles.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of N, S co-doped CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) S2p.
FTIR spectra of l-cystine, l-serine and N, S co-doped CDs.
UV–vis absorption spectra of l-cystine, l-serine and N, S co-doped CDs.

(a)–(b) PL emission spectra (recorded from 320 to 600nm in 20nm increments) of N, S co-doped CDs. (c) PL excitation spectrum of N, S co-doped CDs monitored at 610nm. (d) The corresponding color coordinate of the as-obtained samples excited by 540nm light.

TEM images of N, S co-doped CDs synthesized from l-cystine and l-proline (a), l-cystine and l-alanine (c), and l-cystine and l-asparagine (e). (b), (d), and (f): the corresponding PL emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths in 20nm increments.

(A) The proposed fluorescent mechanism of N, S co-doped CDs under different excitation wavelengths. (B) Fluorescence decay curves of N, S co-doped CDs excited at 370, 460 and 560nm lasers, respectively.
Cytotoxicity evaluation of N, S co-doped CDs by the MTT-assay method.

Peritoneal macrophages of mice incubated with N, S co-doped CDs were imaged under (a) blue and (b) green light excitations. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of the article.)

head.ce:title: N, S co-doped carbon dots with orange luminescence synthesized through polymerization and carbonization reaction of amino acids

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanomaterials and Chemistry Key Laboratory, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou, Zhejiang 325027, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanomaterials and Chemistry Key Laboratory, Wenzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Hematology, The First Affiliated Hospital of Wenzhou Medical University, Wenzhou, Zhejiang 325027, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Hematology, The First Affiliated Hospital of Wenzhou Medical University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N, S co-doped CDs with orange luminescence were synthesized through one-pot polymerization and carbonization reactions under hydrothermal conditions, using two different amino acids as raw materials.

For practical application, it is highly desirable to obtain carbon dots (CDs) through environmentally benign synthetic route, using green raw materials. On the other hand, at present, most of CDs reported in the literature showed blue, green and yellow emission. Therefore it is still necessary to develop new strategy to obtain CDs with longer wavelength emission in order to expand their application range. Toward this end, in this study, N, S co-doped CDs were synthesized through one-pot condensation polymerization and carbonization reactions under hydrothermal conditions, using two different amino acids as raw materials. Taking the reaction of l-serine with l-cystine as an example, the as-obtained products were characterized by various techniques such as transmission electron microscopy, elemental analysis, Fourier-transform infrared spectrum, X-ray photoelectron spectra, and so on. Interestingly, N, S co-doped CDs displayed unique orange emission at room temperature. The possible photoluminescence mechanism of N, S co-doped CDs was proposed. Furthermore, the as-synthesized N, S co-doped CDs were directly applied in the imaging of peritoneal macrophages of mice.

ce:section.ce:para: CDs were accidentally obtained during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes through preparative electrophoresis in 2004 [1]. Since then they have attracted much attention because of their unique physical and chemical properties such as strong fluorescence [2], easy surface functionalization [3], high resistance to photobleaching [4], dual characters as excellent electron donors and acceptors [5,6], low toxicity and good biocompatibility [7,8]. As a rising star, various methods have been developed to prepare this kind of new carbon nanomaterials including electrochemical synthesis [9], combustion oxidation [10], thermal decomposition [11], microwave pyrolysis [12], laser ablation [13], hydrothermal and solvothermal route [14,15]. However, above-mentioned some approaches often involve harsh reaction conditions, long treatment time, and multistep separation processes [16]. For practical application, especially in the case of biomedicine, it is highly desirable to synthesize uniform CDs through environmentally benign reaction route, using green raw materials. More recently, several kinds of natural raw materials such as chicken eggs [17], grass [18], and cocoon silks have been successfully used to synthesize CDs [19]. As the structural units of proteins, amino acids comprise the second-largest component (water is the largest) of human muscles, cells and other tissues. Outside proteins, amino acids also play critical roles in processes such as neurotransmitter transport and biosynthesis. Therefore amino acids can be considered as ideal green raw materials to synthesize various functional materials as well [20]. As we know, dehydration and condensation polymerization reactions between different amino acids will form polymer chains called peptides. Furthermore, the dehydration process of organic compounds will often cause their carbonization under hydrothermal condition [21]. Above all of facts inspire us to hydrothermally synthesize CDs through selecting optimal synthetic conditions, using various amino acids as raw materials.

Up to now, most of CDs reported show blue, green and yellow photoluminescence under efficient excitation. CDs with emission of long wavelength (λ >600nm) are still rare [22,23]. In our previous study, we have found that the doping of nonmetal elements such as N, S and B could adjust the emission of CDs [24]. However, the single-doping of N or S in CDs cannot significantly shift its emission wavelength to longer wavelength direction. Recently, Dong et al. reported that CDs co-doped with N and S prepared through hydrothermal route showed unique excitation-independent violet-blue luminescence [25]. Yet N, S co-doped CDs obtained through sulfuric acid carbonization and etching of hair fiber by Sun et al. displayed blue emission [26]. It was found that the photoluminescence emission was shifted to longer wavelength direction with increasing S content. More recently, graphene quantum dots containing N and S elements prepared with polythiophene derivatives displayed strong deep-red emission [27]. Above studies suggest that it is possible to further obtain CDs with unique luminescence by tuning doping behavior of N and S through design of new synthetic routes.

In this study, we reported one-pot hydrothermal route to synthesize N and S co-doped CDs, using l-serine and l-cystine as raw materials. The l-serine serves as carbon and nitrogen source, while l-cystine provides carbon, nitrogen and sulfur. Unlike previously reported CDs and doped CDs, the as-obtained N, S co-doped CDs exhibited unique orange emission. N, S co-doped CDs with orange emission could also be obtained when employing other amino acid pairs as raw materials, which showed certain generality of the present methods. Based on experimental results, the photoluminescence mechanism of N, S co-doped CDs was tentatively proposed. In addition, N, S co-doped CDs were directly applied in the imaging of peritoneal macrophages of mice in order to demonstrate one of practical applications of the products.

All of amino acids were purchased from Aladdin reagent Inc. and used as received. The dialysis bags were purchased from Solarbio science and technology Inc. (retained molecular weight: 1000Da). In all experimental processes, water used was ultrapure water unless otherwise stated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N, S co-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The N, S co-doped CDs were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal route. In a typical process, 1.2g (5mmol) of l-cystine and 0.53g (5mmol) of l-serine were dispersed into 20mL of ultrapure water, and stirred for several minutes. Then the solution was transferred into 30mL of Teflon lined stainless autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated to 200°C in an electric oven and kept for additional 5h. The final product solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000Da) for 3 days (changed ultrapure water every 8h). The solid powders were collected through freeze drying. The resultant solid products can be easily redispersed into water. For other N, S co-doped CDs, they were prepared through similar procedures except that l-cystine and l-serine pairs were replaced with l-cystine and l-proline, l-cystine and l-alanine, l-cystine and l-asparagine ones, respectively.

The measurements of C, N, and S elements were performed on a PE 2400II CHNS/O elemental analyzer. Infrared spectra were obtained on a Bruker EQUINOX 55 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer using KBr pelleting ranged from 4000 to 400cm−1 in transmission mode. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out with a Thermo ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with an exciting source of Al Kα=1253.6eV. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on a JEOL 2010 microscopy, using an accelerating voltage of 200kV. Zeta-potential measurement was carried out on a Zetasizer nano ZS90 (Malvern). UV-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of the samples were recorded on a Shimadzu UV3600 PC spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured using a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon Inc.) equipped with a 150W xenon lamp as the excitation source.

The QY of N, S co-doped CDs was calculated by comparing the integrated PL intensities and absorbance values of the samples (exited at 540nm), using rhodamine B (RhB) dissolved in absolute ethanol as the standard (QY=49%) [28]. The relative QY can be calculated using the below equation:
Φ =
Φ R
Grad
Grad R
n 2
n R 2
where Grad is the gradient obtained from the plot of the integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance and n is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript R represents the reference fluorophore of known QY. In order to minimize re-absorption effects, absorbencies in the 1cm fluorescence cuvette were kept less than 0.05 at the excitation wavelength of 540nm.

The fluorescence lifetime of the N, S co-doped CDs was measured using time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) with the LED (370, 460 and 560nm) equipped on a Jobin–Yvon–Horiba Fluorometer 4. The emission wavelength of N, S co-doped CDs was set at 610nm.

The cellular cytotoxicity of the sample was tested on Hela cells. Hela cells were cultured using established procedures. The cells were seeded in 96-cell plates and grown for 24h. After that, the culture medium was removed and replaced with Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) supplemented with fetal bovine serum containing N, S co-doped CDs with different doses. The cells were incubated for another 24h. Then 20μL of 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) solution (5mg/mL) was added to each well. The 96-well cell plates were further incubated in a 5% CO2 humidified incubator at 37°C for 4h, followed by removing the culture medium with MTT. Then 200μL of dimethysulfoxide was added into each well. The resulting mixture was shaken for ca. 10min at room temperature. The optical density of the mixture was measured at 490nm by a microplate reader. The cell viability was calculated by assuming 100% viability in the control set without any N, S co-doped CDs.

Four C57BL/6 mice with ages of 6–8 weeks were divided into two groups. A group of mice was supplied with 0.5mL of N, S co-doped CDs saline through intraperitoneal injection. B group was only provided with the same amount of blank saline. Mice were sacrificed by cervical dislocation after 2 days, and then they were disinfected by 75% of ethanol solution for 5min. Subsequently, 8mL of serum-free DMEM culture solution was injected into the abdomens of mice. Standing for about 5min, then 4mL of ascitic fluid was extracted through a syringe under aseptic conditions. After separated centrifugally and washed, they were counted under microscopy. The conventional DMEM (including 10% fetal bovine serum, penicillin 100U/mL, streptomycin 100μg/mL) was used to adjust the cells to desired concentration about 105/mL. The cells were cultured in 96-well cell culture plate in a 5% CO2 humidified incubator at 37°C for 24h. Then the cells were imaged and observed on a conventional inverted fluorescence microscopy equipped with different filters.

For the sake of succinctness, we selected N, S co-doped CDs synthesized through l-cystine and l-serine for detailed study. Fig. 1 illustrates the formation process of N, S co-doped CDs. Under hydrothermal conditions, dehydration and condensation polymerization will occur between l-serine and l-cystine molecules through well-known amidation reaction in the initial stage. Then the resulting peptide chains were further carbonized with prolonged reaction time. Finally, N, S co-doped carbon clusters were grown into bigger N, S co-doped CDs.

Fig. 2a represents TEM image of the final products. TEM observations showed that the sample was well-dispersed nanoparticles with very small size. According to TEM image analysis using Nano Measure software, the statistical particle size distribution of the sample (Fig. 2b) was mainly in the range of 2–3nm and has an average diameter of 2.6nm (100 random nanoparticles were selected for the sample). HRTEM images recorded on these nanoparticles showed that the products were amorphous (Supplementary Data, Fig. S1).

The elemental analysis showed that the sample contained C, N, and S elements. The quantitative analysis showed that the molar atomic ratios of C:N:S in the sample was ca. 80.4:14.2:5.4. According to the above results, the samples can be temporarily called N, S co-doped CDs. To further investigate the surface composition and chemical state of N, S co-doped CDs, XPS was performed (Fig. 3 ). The full scan XPS spectrum indicates that the sample consists of C, N, S, O, Ca and Cl elements. Because the sample was moisture absorbent especially, CaCl2 was used as a drying agent. Therefore Ca and Cl elements existed in the sample. Three peaks located at ca. 285eV, 398.5eV and 164eV correspond to C1s, N1s and S2p, respectively, which can be discerned clearly. The high resolution spectrum of C1s can be fitted into three main wide peaks centered at 284.8, 286.2 and 288.4eV, which are respectively attributed to CC/CC, CN/CS, and CO bonds [25]. The high-resolution spectra of N1s reveal the presence of pyrrolic type N (399.7eV) and N–H groups (401.6eV) N [29]. The high-resolution spectrum of S2p shows two peaks at 163.4 and 164.5eV, which agrees with 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 position of –C–S– covalent bond of the thiophene S [26], respectively.

FTIR spectra of l-cystine, l-serine and N, S co-doped CDs (Fig. 4 ) show that the characteristic absorption vibration peaks of l-cystine and l-serine disappeared after condensation polymerization and carbonization reactions under hydrothermal conditions. The newly emerging peaks of N, S co-doped CDs at 1706, 1647, 1584, 1396, 1227, 1119 and 1024cm−1 can be assigned to CO, CN, CC, CN, CO, CS and CO bonds, respectively. The broad band at 3131–3401cm−1 is ascribed to O–H and N–H bonds. The peaks of 645 and 604cm−1 at lower wave number are attributed to C–S bonds. These results indicated that N and S elements were efficiently doped into carbon framework of CDs, which are consistent with XPS analysis.

Fig. 5 describes the UV–vis absorption spectra of l-cystine, l-serine and N, S co-doped CDs aqueous solution. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of original N, S co-doped CDs aqueous solution did not show absorption peak (Fig. S2). In order to achieve the details of electronic structure of N, S co-doped CDs, it was diluted about 100 fold. The corresponding absorption spectrum was shown in Fig. 5. The absorption of l-serine is below 238nm while l-cystine has two weak absorption peaks located at 250 and 309nm. The absorption of N, S co-doped CDs is obviously different with that of l-cystine and l-serine molecules. There is a sharp absorption peak at 229nm and a wide one at 319nm, which are ascribed to π–π* and n–π* transitions of CC and CO bonds, respectively [30].

Nevertheless, the N, S co-doped CDs solution showed no obviously detectable PL signal when it was excited at 229 and 319nm, respectively. The results showed that inherent conjugated structures were not efficient emission center. With increasing excitation wavelength from 320 to 420nm, as shown in Fig. 6a, the sample shows more and more strong PL emission with invariable peak located at 598nm. Further increasing excitation wavelength, the emission peaks of the sample would move to longer wavelength direction (Fig. 6b). The luminescent intensity reached a maximum at emission wavelength of 610nm when excitation wavelength was adjusted to 540nm. The excitation spectrum monitored at 610nm showed a wide peak centered at 540nm (Fig. 6c), which also further supported the results of PL emission. The excitation spectrum of N, S co-doped CDs was obviously different from corresponding UV–vis absorption spectrum, which also showed distinctive PL origin. The Commission International d’Eclairage coordinate of the sample excited at 540nm is X =0.607 and Y =0.393. As shown in Commission International d’Eclairage chromaticity diagram (Fig. 6d), the sample shows orange emission. Using RhB as a reference dye, the relative QY of N, S co-doped CDs can reach 7%. It should be pointed out that the present N, S co-doped CDs with orange emission can keep good stability in solution without apparent coagulation and reduction of PL emission intensity for at least 6 months. The zeta-potential measurement (Fig. S3) shows that N, S co-doped CDs dispersed in near neutral water exhibits negative charges, which can be attributed to good stability of the sample. The negative charges on the surfaces of N, S co-doped CDs mainly come from carboxylate ions, which can be confirmed from the results of XPS and FTIR.

Both l-cystine and l-serine are necessary to obtain uniform N, S co-doped CDs. When only l-cystine was used, irregular nanoparticles were produced (Fig. S4). Although they can also emit orange fluorescence (Fig. S5), their PL efficiency is low (QY=4.1, Table S1). The products became short nanorods in the case of l-serine (Fig. S6). The strongest emission peak of the products was moved to short wavelength and located at 551nm (Fig. S7). Interestingly, when l-cystine and l-serine pairs were replaced with l-cystine and l-proline, l-cystine and l-alanine, l-cystine and l-asparagine ones, respectively, the corresponding products became N, S co-doped CDs with average size of 5 (Fig. 7a), 9 (Fig. 7c) and 13nm (Fig. 7e). All of the products show the strongest emission at orange wavelength range (Fig. 7b, d and f). The results suggest that the present method has certain generality in aspect of synthesizing N, S co-doped CDs with orange emission.

At present, the exact fluorescent mechanism of N, S co-doped CDs is still unclear. Dong et al. believed that the doping of nitrogen could introduce the CDs a new kind of surface state, whose density could be increased dramatically by the co-doped sulfur atoms [25]. Sun and co-authors found that structural changes caused by doping with N and S heteroatoms effectively tuned the intrinsic properties of CDs, including electronic properties as well as surface states. They observed that the emission peaks of N, S co-doped CDs with increasing S content shifted to longer wavelengths when excited by the same wavelength [26]. For our N, S co-doped CDs, as described by the experimental results above, no detectable PL signal was observed when excited at the absorption peaks, suggesting that the PL does not originate from fluorophores within the carbogenic core [31]. When the synthetic time was set at 2 and 16h, the sizes of the resulting N, S co-doped CDs are 2.8 and 46.3nm (Fig. S8), respectively. The corresponding PL emission peaks are located at 622 and 595nm (Fig. S9). The results showed that quantum-confinement effects were not the key parameter that determines their PL properties [9], either. Otherwise, the PL emission peaks of N, S co-doped CDs would take place redshift with increased sizes. In addition, the S contents in the samples respectively obtained at 2 and 16h are about the same. Therefore S content is not the main reason for PL redshift of CDs, either. When only l-serine was used, N-doped nanoparticles were obtained. But, the N-doped nanoparticles emit green fluorescence (Fig. S7). The result suggests that the synergistic doping effect of N and S is responsible for unique orange emission of CDs. More recently, it was found that the incorporation of S into CDs could introduce S-related energy levels between π and π* of C and the resulting additional energy levels lead to multiple emission peaks [32]. Considering all the above facts, a tentative mechanism is proposed to explain the orange emission of N, S co-doped CDs. Both S and N in CDs introduce additional energy levels between π and π* of carbon. Under the excitation of short wavelength, the excited electrons jumped into higher energy levels. After vibration relaxation and interband crossing, the radiative recombination will then take place on Sπ* and give rise to excitation-independent orange luminescence (route a). However, with increasing excitation wavelength, electron transition will carry through at lower energy levels. Without additional interband crossing, orange luminescence was directly emitted after vibration relaxation (route b). The possible PL emission mechanism of N, S co-doped CDs was schematically shown in Fig. 8A. PL decays of N, S co-doped CDs under different excitation wavelengths were shown in Fig. 8B. All the curves could be well fitted to a two-exponential model. The fluorescence lifetimes of N, S co-doped CDs excited at 370, 460 and 560nm are τ 1 =1.8ns and τ 2 =34.8ns, τ 1 =0.7ns and τ 2 =2.4ns, τ 1 =0.5ns and τ 2 =2.5ns (Table S2), respectively. The lifetime of the sample excited at short wavelength is obviously different that done at longer wavelength. The difference in lifetime probably originates from additional energy levels caused by N and S co-doping, which also supports the proposed PL emission mechanism.

For practical biological applications, the toxicity of N, S co-doped CDs must be firstly concerned. Therefore, the cytotoxicity of N, S co-doped CDs synthesized from l-cystine and l-serine was evaluated using HeLa cells with MTT viability assay. As shown in Fig. 9 , N, S co-doped CDs have negligible toxicity to HeLa cells, even at high concentration of 0.6mg/mL. It is worthwhile pointing out that the concentration of 0.6mg/mL is much higher than those required for potential applications such as optical imaging of living cells, biosensor, and biomedicine. Moreover, the toxicity has no correlation with the concentration of CDs, and this character differs greatly from conventional quantum dots whose toxicity is concentration-dependent [33]. This result suggests that the as-synthesized N, S co-doped CDs are suitable for large-scale application.

In order to demonstrate that the N, S co-doped CDs can be used for biological application, they were directly applied in the imaging of peritoneal macrophages of mice without any further functionalization. The as-synthesized N, S co-doped CDs dispersed in saline were injected into the abdominal cavity of mice. Then the peritoneal macrophages of mice were extracted, isolated, and further cultured. The final peritoneal macrophages of mice were imaged under conventional fluorescence microscopy. As shown in Fig. 10b, peritoneal macrophages of mice become orange when they were excited by green light. However, these cells do not show color when they were excited by blue light (Fig. 10a). The results are in agreement with those of PL spectra. In addition, the peritoneal macrophages of mice are colorless in the control sample where no N, S co-doped CDs were used (data not shown here). It means that the N, S co-doped CDs had penetrated into the peritoneal macrophages of mice. Furthermore, the photoluminescence intensity of the labeled macrophages showed no obvious reduction after continuous excitation for 20min, suggesting that N, S co-doped CDs possessed a remarkable photostability and low photobleaching. These results suggest that the present N, S co-doped CDs have promising applications in cell labeling.

In conclusion, uniform N, S co-doped CDs have been hydrothermally synthesized from two kinds of common amino acids, namely, l-serine and l-cystine through polymerization and carbonization reactions. Selecting other different amino acids pairs as raw materials, N, S co-doped CDs could also be obtained, suggesting the generality of the present method. Interestingly, the as-synthesized N, S co-doped CDs show unique orange fluorescence, which is obviously different from previous reports. Based on the experimental results, the synergistic doping effect of N and S was responsible for unique orange emission of CDs. The additional energy levels between π and π* of carbon caused by N and S doping may tune the optical properties. However, the exact orange PL mechanism of N, S co-doped CDs still need to be further studied. Green and scalable synthetic method, low cytotoxicity, good photostability, and unique orange emission make N, S co-doped CDs promising application in many fields such as biomedicine, catalysis, and optoelectronics.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to express acknowledgement for partial financial support from NSFC (51372173, 51002107, and 21173159), NSFC for Distinguished Young Scholars (51025207), Research Climb Plan of ZJED (pd2013383), and Opening Project of State Key Laboratory of High Performance Ceramics and Superfine Microstructure (SKL201409SIC).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2015.03.029.
